name: Chaotic inflation
summary: Inflation begins from generic chaotic initial conditions in which the inflaton field takes an arbitrary large value at the Planck scale; for a wide class of polynomial potentials V(phi) = lambda phi^n, slow-roll inflation occurs simply because phi >> M_Pl. Solves the initial-conditions problem of new inflation by not requiring a special low-entropy starting state.

addresses_puzzles.note: Predicts r > 0.1 for V ~ phi^2 (now excluded by BICEP/Keck) and r ~ 0.05 for V ~ phi^4/3 (alpha-attractor-like; tightly constrained but not closed). The 'chaotic' initial condition argument remains compelling but specific potentials are pressured.

touches_benchmarks.note: V = m^2 phi^2/2 chaotic inflation predicts r ~ 0.13, ruled out by BICEP/Keck r < 0.036. Lower-power variants survive in narrow windows.
Predicts n_s ~ 1 - 2/N for V ~ phi^2 with N ~ 60 e-folds, in marginal tension with Planck.

new_scales: m ~ 10^13 GeV (for quadratic potential to give Planck-scale CMB amplitude)
introduces.note: Inflaton mass / coupling and number of e-folds (set by reheating).

history: Linde (1983), 'Chaotic inflation', Phys. Lett. B 129, 177-181 — proposed the scenario, including its insensitivity to initial conditions. m^2 phi^2 chaotic inflation was the dominant simple model through the 2000s; ruled out by Planck + BICEP/Keck post-2014.
